summary: Jeff Atwood argues that touch interfaces are the inevitable future of computing, but warns that touchscreen-only devices create barriers to writing that could harm communication. He reviews the Microsoft Surface RT as a breakthrough device that successfully combines touch with a keyboard, solving the text input problem that plagues tablets. Despite its first-generation flaws, Atwood sees Surface's form factor as the future of laptops, proving that touch and typing can work harmoniously together. The post reflects on how the natural human desire to touch screens has been validated by the tablet era, while expressing concern about a generation locked into minimal communication by on-screen keyboards.
key_points: Touch interfaces represent the natural evolution of computing, reducing devices to their absolute minimum—a flat slate that invites interaction
Touchscreen-only devices discourage writing paragraphs, creating a 'major regression' that could lock billions into minimal communication
The Surface RT proves that touch and keyboard can work harmoniously together, making it a 'laptop killer' rather than a tablet killer
First-generation devices like Surface RT offer glimpses of the future but will be obsolete within two years
The 'gorilla arm' argument against touchscreens on laptops has been proven false—typing and touching are 'spectacularly compatible'
money_quotes: My strategy with new computing devices is simple: I buy 'em all, then try living with them.
Welcome to the future, here's your… rectangle.
If it doesn't have a keyboard, I feel that my thoughts are being forced out through a straw.
Anything that adds a significant barrier to the act of placing words on a page is kind of dangerous.

Surface is just like the first iPad in that it has all the flaws and rough edges you'd expect in a version one device. But it is also like the first iPad in that there is undeniably the core of something revelatory and transformative here.
Reviewers think Surface is intended to be a tablet killer, but it isn't. It's a laptop killer.
Surface proves that's not true; typing and touching are spectacularly compatible, at least for laptops.
Do not buy this device expecting longevity. Buy it because you want to see tomorrow today.
key_insight: The future of computing lies not in choosing between touch and keyboard, but in combining them—and devices that force users to abandon writing represent a dangerous regression for human communication.
